standard: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)

observations: The label's programmatic relationship to its form control is what this criterion actually turns on: either `for` must match the control's `id` (tag="label"), or the label must be rendered as an ancestor wrapping the control, or a `<fieldset>`'s `<legend>` (tag="legend") must precede its grouped controls. `ct-label` renders only the label/legend text itself, not the control — establishing and maintaining that `for`/`id` match (or DOM wrapping) is the implementer's responsibility. A visually-adjacent but programmatically unconnected label fails this criterion even though it looks associated.
The `autocomplete` attribute this criterion concerns belongs on the associated input, not on the label itself.
The required-field marker is conveyed through both a distinct color (`--ct-label-*-required-color`) and visible text (`required-text`, defaulting to "(required)") — not color alone.
Both the base label color and the required-marker color meet the minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against their light/dark theme backgrounds.
Text is still visible and readable when resized to at least 200%.
Text is visible/readable when the line spacing is at least 1.5 times the font size, letter spacing is at least 0.12 times the font size, and word spacing is at least 0.16 times the font size.

standard: 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (Level AA)

observations: Native `<label>` behavior means clicking anywhere on the label moves focus to (and, for checkboxes/radios, activates) its associated control — but only when `for` correctly matches the control's `id`. This is an implementer responsibility, not something `ct-label` can guarantee on its own since it doesn't render the control.

standard: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)

observations: The label element itself is not focusable; only its associated control receives focus, and that control's position in the focus order is outside this component's scope.
Compliance depends on the implementer supplying `content` that specifically and clearly names the associated field's purpose (e.g. "Email address", not "Field 3" or a repeated generic string).
The label receives no focus of its own; the associated control's focus-visible styling is out of this component's scope.

If the implementer also sets an `aria-label`/`aria-labelledby` on the associated control that overrides its accessible name, that accessible name must still contain this label's visible `content` text verbatim (e.g. a speech-input user saying the visible label should be able to target the control). Simplest compliant approach: let the native `for`/`id` association supply the accessible name and don't add a conflicting `aria-label` on the control at all.

Not a target itself, but a correctly-associated label (`for`/`id` match or wrapping) extends the associated control's effective clickable/tappable area for free, which helps this criterion for the control — worth noting to implementers pairing `ct-label` with a small checkbox/radio.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting the correct lang attribute on the html element.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting lang attributes on parts of the page that differ from the main language.
The label is not focusable, so it cannot itself trigger an on-focus context change.
Compliance depends on the implementer using the same label text for the same kind of field everywhere it recurs across the site (e.g. always "Email address", never varying it page to page).
Field-level error identification is `ct-field-message`'s responsibility, not the label's — though a required field left empty is often reported against the label's associated control.

Providing a text label — and, via `required`/`required-text`, a required-field instruction — for a form field is this component's entire purpose. It satisfies this criterion directly once correctly associated with its control, rather than leaving labelling to the implementer to add separately.

Native `<label>`/`<legend>` semantics give assistive technology the correct role automatically, but the accessible name is only correctly exposed on the *control* when `for` (tag="label") references a valid, matching `id`, or the `<legend>` precedes its `<fieldset>`'s controls (tag="legend") — `ct-label` cannot enforce either since it doesn't render the control/fieldset itself. Marking a field `required` here is a visual/textual cue only; the implementer must still set `required`/`aria-required="true"` on the actual control for the requirement to be exposed programmatically.
